meta.title: Adaptation of Aspergillus niger to several antifungal agents -- Park et al. 140 (9): 2409 -- Microbiology

abstractHtml: Adaptation of Aspergillus niger to short-term stress induced by three antifungal agents [amphotericin B (AMPH), miconazole (MCZ), and ketoconazole (KCZ)] was observed and evaluated quantitatively using individual hyphae. Spores were inoculated onto a poly-L-lysine-coated glass plate making up the base of a culture vessel. Potato dextrose broth (PDB) was added and the vessel incubated for 24 h at 28 degrees C. The growth rate of an arbitrarily selected test hypha was measured automatically. Exposure to AMPH (0.075 micrograms ml-1) stopped the growth of the hypha. After washing with PDB, the same concentration of AMPH was applied again. The growth of the test hypha was not inhibited. This phenomenon was defined as adaptation to the short-term stress of AMPH. Similarly, adaptation was observed with MCZ (0.01 microgram ml-1) and KCZ (0.5 microgram ml-1). The time required for the test hypha to restart growth after washing with PDB depended upon the concentration of MCZ or KCZ, but not upon the concentration of AMPH.
